For EACH email provide: Send timing, Subject line (A/B variant), Preview text (40 chars), Body (150-200 words, conversational, one clear CTA), CTA button text, Goal. Email sequence: 1. Welcome + quick start (Immediate) 2. First value moment (+1 day) 3. Social proof (+3 days) 4. Pro tip (+7 days) 5. Check-in + upgrade nudge (+14 days) Product: ${inf.name} | Price: ${inf.price} | Revenue: ${inf.revenue_model}`, 3000, 'budget'), aiRaw(`Generate a customer discovery interview script for "${inf.name}" (${inf.type_label}). This script is for the founder's FIRST 10 CUSTOMER INTERVIEWS. Goal: validate product-market fit. Include: ## Interview Setup (who, how to recruit, logistics) ## Interview Script (20 min): Warm-up (2 min, 2 questions), Problem Discovery (8 min, 5 open-ended questions + probes), Solution Validation (5 min, 3 questions + price sensitivity), Closing (5 min, referrals + thanks) ## Scoring Rubric (5 signals, strong vs weak PMF) ## After 10 Interviews (proceed/pivot/kill framework) Product: ${inf.name} | Target: ${inf.acquisition} | Price: ${inf.price}`, 3000, 'budget'), ]; if (RESEARCH_DATA?.competitors?.length) { const compList = RESEARCH_DATA.competitors.slice(0, 5).map(c => `- ${c.title}: ${c.url} — ${(c.snippet||'').slice(0, 100)}`).join('\n'); _edcPromises.push( aiRaw(`Generate a structured competitive analysis for "${inf.name}" (${inf.type_label}). Known competitors from research: ${compList} For EACH competitor (up to 5): Name, URL, Pricing, Key Strength, Key Weakness, Our Advantage over them. Then provide: ## Positioning Matrix (2x2, place all competitors + us) ## Key Takeaways (3 bullets: copy, avoid, gap) Product: ${inf.name} | Revenue: ${inf.revenue_model} at ${inf.price}`, 3000, 'standard') ); } const [_emailsR, _discoveryR, _competeR] = await Promise.allSettled(_edcPromises); const _emailRaw = _emailsR?.status === 'fulfilled' ? _emailsR.value : ''; if (_emailRaw?.length > 300) { files['docs/EMAIL-SEQUENCES.md'] = `# Email Onboarding Sequence — ${inf.name}\n\n> 5-email welcome drip for new customers\n> Generated: ${new Date().toLocaleDateString('en-US',{year:'numeric',month:'long',day:'numeric'})}\n\n${_emailRaw}`; setP('emails', 'ok', '5-email sequence ready'); } else { setP('emails', 'sk', 'Email sequence skipped'); } const _discRaw = _discoveryR?.status === 'fulfilled' ? _discoveryR.value : ''; if (_discRaw?.length > 300) { files['docs/CUSTOMER-DISCOVERY.md'] = `# Customer Discovery Script — ${inf.name}\n\n> Interview guide for your first 10 customers\n> Generated: ${new Date().toLocaleDateString('en-US',{year:'numeric',month:'long',day:'numeric'})}\n\n${_discRaw}`; setP('discovery', 'ok', 'Interview script + scoring rubric ready'); } else { setP('discovery', 'sk', 'Discovery script skipped'); } if (RESEARCH_DATA?.competitors?.length && _competeR) { const _compRaw = _competeR?.status === 'fulfilled' ? _competeR.value : ''; if (_compRaw?.length > 300) { files['docs/COMPETITIVE-ANALYSIS.md'] = `# Competitive Teardown — ${inf.name}\n\n> Competitive intelligence\n> Generated: ${new Date().toLocaleDateString('en-US',{year:'numeric',month:'long',day:'numeric'})}\n\n${_compRaw}`; setP('compete', 'ok', `${RESEARCH_DATA.competitors.slice(0,5).length} competitors analyzed`); } else { setP('compete', 'sk', 'Competitive analysis skipped'); } } } // ── PHASE: Business Depth Documents (78 gaps implementation) ──────────── // Generates 78 additional business documents covering MBA frameworks, // accelerator playbooks, serial entrepreneur patterns, legal templates, // financial depth, GTM tactics, product strategy, and people/culture. // Documents are generated in parallel batches using Promise.allSettled. updateGlobalProgress(9, 14, 'Phase 9 of 14 · Business Depth Documents'); let depthDocsGenerated = 0; if (_v4SkipHeavy) { addP('depth-docs', 'Business depth documents', 'Skipped (Demo / Express — lean bundle)'); setP('depth-docs', 'sk', 'Full ~78-doc depth pack deferred; switch archetype for complete export'); } else { addP('depth-docs', 'Generating business depth documents', '78 documents across 8 domains'); setP('depth-docs', 'run'); // ── CRITICAL: 9 foundational business documents ──────────────────────── const criticalDocs = [ ['PMF-MEASUREMENT.md', 'pmf', 'PMF Measurement', `Generate a Product-Market Fit measurement framework for "${inf.name}" (${inf.type_label}, ${inf.revenue_model} at ${inf.price}). ${desc}\n\nInclude:\n1. SEAN ELLIS TEST: The exact "very disappointed" survey (4 questions), how to distribute it, sample size requirements (min 40 responses), and the 40% threshold interpretation with examples.\n2. PMF SCORING METHODOLOGY: Create a composite PMF score from 5 signals: Sean Ellis %, retention rate, organic growth rate, NPS, and usage frequency. Weight each signal and define scoring thresholds (no PMF / emerging / strong / exceptional).\n3. WEEKLY PMF TRACKING TEMPLATE: Dashboard with 8 metrics tracked weekly with targets.\n4. PIVOT-OR-PERSEVERE DECISION TREE: Quantitative decision framework — if metrics X below threshold Y for Z weeks, take action A.\n5. INNOVATION ACCOUNTING: Define 3 levels — establish baseline, tune engine, pivot/persevere. Create specific dashboard for this business.\n6. LEADING VS LAGGING INDICATORS: Identify 5 leading indicators that predict PMF 30-60 days ahead for ${inf.type_label} businesses.`], ['UNSCALABLE-PLAYBOOK.md', 'unscalable', 'Unscalable Playbook', `Generate a "Do Things That Don't Scale" checklist for "${inf.name}" (${inf.type_label}, ${inf.revenue_model} at ${inf.price}). ${desc}\n\nBased on Paul Graham's essay. Include 20 specific manual, high-touch actions organized by:\n\nCUSTOMER ACQUISITION (5 actions): personal outreach, hand-delivered demos, manual onboarding calls, founder-led sales, handwritten notes.\nONBOARDING (4 actions): personal setup calls, white-glove configuration, custom walkthroughs, first-week daily check-ins.\nSUPPORT (4 actions): founder-answers-every-ticket, proactive check-ins, screen-share debugging, customer Slack channels.\nSALES (4 actions): custom proposals, in-person meetings, hand-crafted demos, personal follow-ups.\nPRODUCT (3 actions): manual data imports, concierge fulfillment, manual feature requests.\n\nFor EACH action: what to do manually, when to automate (specific metric trigger like "when you have 50+ customers"), what automation looks like, and estimated time savings.`], ['docs/OPERATING-AGREEMENT-TEMPLATE.md', 'op-agree', 'Operating Agreement', `Generate an LLC Operating Agreement template for "${inf.name}". Include: Article 1 Formation (name, purpose, registered agent, term), Article 2 Members (initial members table, additional members, membership interests), Article 3 Capital (initial contributions, additional contributions, capital accounts, no interest on capital), Article 4 Profit/Loss Distribution (allocation, distribution timing, tax distributions), Article 5 Management (member-managed vs manager-managed, voting, meetings, quorum, written consent), Article 6 Transfer Restrictions (right of first refusal, permitted transfers, drag-along, tag-along), Article 7 Dissolution (events, winding up, distribution of assets), Article 8 Dispute Resolution (mediation then arbitration, governing law), Article 9 Buy-Sell (death/disability, voluntary withdrawal, valuation method, payment terms), Article 10 Non-Compete. Add [BRACKETED PLACEHOLDERS] for customization. Disclaimer: review with legal counsel.`], ['docs/IP-ASSIGNMENT.md', 'ip-assign', 'IP Assignment', `Generate an IP Assignment Agreement template for "${inf.name}". Cover: 1) ASSIGNMENT clause — all work product, inventions, improvements assigned to company, 2) WORK-FOR-HIRE — designation under Copyright Act, 3) MORAL RIGHTS WAIVER, 4) REPRESENTATIONS — original work, no conflicts, no prior encumbrances, 5) PRIOR INVENTIONS SCHEDULE — list of pre-existing IP excluded from assignment, 6) FURTHER ASSURANCES — obligation to sign future documents, 7) CONSIDERATION — acknowledgment of adequate consideration. Also include: explanation of contractor vs employee IP ownership differences, work-for-hire doctrine summary, and why this agreement is critical (the legal time bomb analogy). Format as ready-to-use template with [BRACKETS].`], ['ENTITY-SELECTION.md', 'entity', 'Entity Selection', `Generate an Entity Selection Analysis for "${inf.name}" (${inf.type_label}, ${inf.revenue_model} at ${inf.price}). ${desc}\n\nCompare across 15 dimensions in a matrix:\n| Dimension | LLC | S-Corp | C-Corp (DE) |\nCover: liability protection, taxation (pass-through vs corporate), self-employment tax, fundraising compatibility, equity compensation (options/RSUs), administrative burden, annual costs, exit treatment, investors' preference, QSBS eligibility, number of shareholders, ownership restrictions, formation complexity, operating flexibility, public perception.\n\nInclude DECISION TREE: if raising VC → C-Corp. If bootstrapped <$500K → LLC. If profitable >$100K → S-Corp election analysis. If planning exit in 5 years → C-Corp for QSBS.\n\nQSBS Section 1202: qualification requirements, $10M exclusion, 5-year holding period, eligible businesses.\n\nState analysis: Delaware vs Wyoming vs home state. Franchise tax comparison.\n\nROBS PATHWAY (Rollover for Business Startup — qualified plan capitalization):\n- Define ROBS accurately: retirement assets rolled into a NEW qualified plan (typically a 401(k) sponsored by a new operating C corporation); the plan uses those funds to purchase employer stock of that C corp, which injects capital into the business. This is structurally different from "many LLCs rolling up into an S-Corp" — ROBS is ERISA/DOL-regulated and centers on corporate stock held by the plan.\n- Why a C corporation is typical: S corporation rules restrict certain retirement-plan ownership structures; practical ROBS implementations overwhelmingly use a C corp as the operating entity issuing qualifying employer securities to the plan.\n- Balanced economics: corporate-level tax still applies where applicable; founder W-2 compensation is taxable; distributions and liquidity events have tax character depending on facts — avoid claiming "100% of profit is tax-free forever" without nuance. Explain tax-deferred growth INSIDE the qualified plan and the distinction between plan investments vs personal cash flow.\n- Compliance pillars: bona fide plan, prohibited transaction rules, fair market value for stock, ongoing administration (e.g. Form 5500), fiduciary duty, reasonable compensation if the founder is also an employee.\n- Fit assessment for ${inf.name}: when ROBS is worth exploring (substantial rollover capital, willingness to run corporate formalities, long horizon) vs when LLC/S-Corp/C-Corp without ROBS is simpler.\n- Risks: setup and annual admin costs, IRS/DOL scrutiny if informal, exit complexity when the plan holds a large equity block.\n- REQUIRED disclaimer block: ROBS requires a specialized ERISA-qualified administrator and legal counsel; this analysis is educational only, not tax or legal advice.`], ['RUNWAY-CALCULATOR.md', 'runway', 'Runway Calculator', `Generate a Cash Flow Runway Calculator for "${inf.name}" (${inf.type_label}, ${inf.revenue_model} at ${inf.price}). ${desc}\n\nInclude:\n1. BURN RATE TEMPLATE: Monthly expense breakdown (team, infrastructure, marketing, legal, office, misc). Define gross burn (total spend) vs net burn (spend minus revenue). Show why the distinction matters.\n2. RUNWAY FORMULA: Runway = Cash / Net Monthly Burn. Calculate for $50K, $100K, $250K, $500K, $1M starting cash.\n3. DEFAULT ALIVE/DEAD ASSESSMENT: Paul Graham's framework — if current revenue growth rate continues, will you become profitable before running out of cash? Show the calculation.\n4. RUNWAY EXTENSION STRATEGIES: 10 specific tactics (cut non-essential spend, renegotiate contracts, accelerate collections, bridge financing, revenue sprints, hiring freeze, convert fixed to variable, prepaid annual plans, government grants, factoring).\n5. FUNDING TIMELINE: When to start raising based on runway (start at 6 months remaining, expect 3-6 month process).\n6. 12-MONTH CASH FLOW TEMPLATE: Monthly projection with revenue, expenses, net cash flow, cumulative cash, runway remaining.\n7. BREAK-EVEN ANALYSIS: Fixed costs, variable costs per customer, contribution margin, break-even customer count at ${inf.price}.`], ['CUSTOMER-SUCCESS.md', 'cust-success', 'Customer Success', `Generate a Customer Success Framework for "${inf.name}" (${inf.type_label}, ${inf.revenue_model} at ${inf.price}). ${desc}\n\nInclude:\n1. POST-SALE JOURNEY MAP: 7 stages — purchase, onboarding (day 1-7), activation (day 7-30), adoption (month 2-3), expansion (month 4-6), renewal (month 11-12), advocacy (ongoing). Define milestones and touchpoints for each.\n2. HEALTH SCORE: Define 5-7 signals with weights — login frequency (20%), feature breadth (20%), support tickets (15%), NPS response (15%), billing status (15%), engagement trend (15%). Score 0-100, define brackets: healthy (70+), at-risk (40-69), critical (<40).\n3. QBR TEMPLATE: Quarterly Business Review agenda — value delivered, usage metrics, ROI calculation, roadmap preview, expansion opportunities, feedback.\n4. EXPANSION STRATEGY: Upsell triggers (usage hitting limits, team growth, feature requests), cross-sell identification, expansion revenue targets (net retention >120%).\n5. CHURN RISK SIGNALS: 8 behavioral signals that predict churn 30-60 days ahead. Early intervention playbooks for each.\n6. FIRST 90 DAYS PLAYBOOK: Day 1, 3, 7, 14, 30, 60, 90 touchpoints with specific actions and communication templates.`], ['HIRING-PLAN.md', 'hiring', 'Hiring Plan', `Generate a Hiring Plan for "${inf.name}" (${inf.type_label}, ${inf.revenue_model} at ${inf.price}). ${desc}\n\nInclude:\n1. FIRST 5 HIRES: Ordered by priority with timing tied to revenue milestones. For each: role title, key responsibilities (5), skills required, salary range, equity range (%), why this hire at this position in sequence, interview process (4 stages).\n2. COMPENSATION BENCHMARKING: Methodology using Levels.fyi, Pave, Carta data. Geographic adjustments. Cash vs equity tradeoff at different stages.\n3. HIRING SCORECARD: Structured interview evaluation template with 8 competencies scored 1-5.\n4. EMPLOYEE ONBOARDING: Day 1/Week 1/Month 1 checklist (20 items each). 30-60-90 day goals template.\n5. CULTURE VALUES: 5 core values with behavioral examples and interview questions to assess each.\n6. HIRE VS CONTRACT DECISION: Framework — when to use contractors vs FTEs based on duration, IP sensitivity, cost, management overhead.\n7. REMOTE HIRING: Where to find candidates (5 sources), screening for remote readiness, onboarding remote employees.`], ['FOUNDER-AGREEMENT.md', 'founder', 'Founder Agreement', `Generate a Founder Operating Agreement for "${inf.name}". This is NOT the legal LLC agreement — it's the operational agreement between co-founders covering how they work together.\n\nInclude:\n1. ROLE MATRIX: CEO/CTO/COO responsibilities clearly delineated. Decision authority by domain (product, engineering, sales, finance, hiring, partnerships).\n2. DECISION FRAMEWORK: What needs consensus (fundraising, pivots, key hires, >$10K spend) vs individual authority (day-to-day operations in your domain).\n3. EQUITY SPLIT RATIONALE: Framework for determining fair split. Factors: idea (5-10%), execution capability (20-30%), domain expertise (15-20%), time commitment (20-30%), capital contribution (10-20%).\n4. VESTING: Standard 4-year / 1-year cliff. Reverse vesting for founder shares. What happens to unvested shares on departure.\n5. TIME COMMITMENT: Expected hours/week, moonlighting policy, outside board seats, competing interests disclosure.\n6. CONFLICT RESOLUTION: 3-tier process — direct conversation (48 hours), mediation with advisor/mentor, binding arbitration.\n7. DEPARTURE SCENARIOS: Voluntary (good leaver — vested shares retained, unvested forfeited), involuntary (bad leaver — board removal process, buyback at fair value or discount), death/disability (life insurance, buyback obligation).\n8. IP & NON-COMPETE: All founder work belongs to company. 12-month non-compete in same market post-departure.\n9. REVIEW CADENCE: Quarterly founder check-in agenda template.`] ]; // Generate critical docs in one parallel batch (all 9 at once) await Promise.allSettled(criticalDocs.map(async ([file, id, label, prompt]) => { addP(id, label, '', '⬡ Critical'); setP(id, 'run'); try { const raw = await aiRaw(prompt, 6000); if (raw?.length > 500) { files[file] = `# ${label} — ${inf.name}\n\n> Generated by Your Deputy Intelligence Engine\n> ${new Date().toLocaleDateString('en-US',{year:'numeric',month:'long',day:'numeric'})}\n\n${raw}`; setP(id, 'ok', `${file} ready`); depthDocsGenerated++; } else { setP(id, 'sk', 'Insufficient content'); } } catch(e) { setP(id, 'sk', e.message); } })); // ── END: Critical business docs (9/78) ──────────── // ── Bank-ready funding system (3 pillars vs broker ~10% success fee) ── if (!_v4SkipHeavy) { addP('bank_funding', 'Bank-ready funding playbook', '', '⬡ Bank funding'); setP('bank_funding', 'run'); try { const raw = await aiRaw(`You are a senior advisor on **small-business and investment-entity capital access** (banks, credit unions, SBA-backed programs, and business-card/line products). The reader already has or is forming an LLC (or similar entity) and may use capital for operations or asset-backed strategies (e.g. real estate) where permitted. ## Context for this business - Name: ${inf.name} - Type: ${inf.type_label} - Model: ${inf.revenue_model} at ${inf.price} - Summary: ${desc} - Viability snapshot: ${V2_SCORECARD ? JSON.stringify({ composite: V2_SCORECARD.composite, verdict: V2_SCORECARD.verdict }).slice(0, 400) : 'N/A'} ## Your job Write **BANK-FUNDING-PLAYBOOK.md** content (markdown body only, no outer title) that explains **how the “three things” system works** — the same logic people pay funding companies ~5–10% success fees for — so a founder can **DIY the thinking** (not replace underwriters, brokers, CPAs, or attorneys). ### 1) Economics: why success fees exist - Explain **success-fee brokers**: they bundle **packaging**, **lender/product sequencing**, and **placement**. On e.g. $150K accessed, a10% fee is painful but reflects **coordination + risk + time** — not “interest.” - Contrast **interest/APR** vs **one-time success fee** vs **equity dilution** in one short table. - **DIY path**: lower cash cost, higher time cost and execution risk; must bring discipline. ### 2) Pillar A — Fundable **people** + **business credit file** (the “invisible” first requirement) - Personal: **strong borrower profile** (payment history, utilization, depth of file, derogatories, PG strength when required). No guarantees — describe what **underwriting systems reward**. - Business: **real separation** — EIN, entity name, business address/phone where applicable, **business bank account**, revenue/expense paper trail, time-in-business effects. - List **common denial reasons** (thin file, high utilization, recent inquiries, DTI, mismatched stated income, entity not “real”). ### 3) Pillar B — **Bank-ready business** (the “platter” this repo helps assemble) - Define **bank-ready** as: coherent story + numbers + legal hygiene + **artifacts underwriters expect**. - **Map explicitly** to these generated artifacts (reference by filename): \`SBA-BUSINESS-PLAN.md\`, \`INVESTOR-READINESS.md\`, \`REVENUE-MODEL.md\`, \`BUSINESS-SYSTEM.md\`, \`ENTITY-SELECTION.md\`, \`RUNWAY-CALCULATOR.md\`, \`FUNDRAISING-STRATEGY.md\`, \`THREE-STATEMENT-MODEL.md\` / cap table docs if present, \`docs/OPERATING-AGREEMENT-TEMPLATE.md\`, formation checklist items. - Add a **“platter checklist”** table: Item | Why the bank cares | Where it lives in this build. ### 4) Pillar C — **Funding sequence** (strategy over brute force) - Explain **sequence** as: order of **product applications**, timing of **hard pulls**, managing **debt-to-income**, avoiding **inquiry sprawl**, and **stabilizing** the file between steps. - Provide a **phase-based playbook** (e.g. Phase 0 stabilize → Phase 1 entity + bank core → Phase 2 starter business products → Phase 3 term/line scale) with **decision gates** (“if denied, stop and fix X before the next app”). - Include **mistakes that mimic broker value** — shotgun apps, mixing personal and business story, weak financials, applying before entity bank trail exists. ### 5) Illustrative scenario (clearly labeled hypothetical) - Walk a **hypothetical** path toward ~$150K **aggregate** exposure across products (not a promise): show how **sequence** and **platter** reduce friction. Use **no** lender names as endorsements; stay educational. ### 6) Compliance block (required) - State this is **not financial, tax, or legal advice**; not a solicitation; **no guarantee** of approvals or amounts; readers must verify with **licensed professionals** and institutions; regulations vary by jurisdiction and product. Tone: direct, founder-friendly, no hype, no “secret hack” language.`, 7000, 'standard'); if (raw?.length > 500) { files['BANK-FUNDING-PLAYBOOK.md'] = `# Bank-Ready Funding Playbook — ${inf.name}\n\n> Three pillars: fundable profile · bank-ready business · funding sequence · DIY vs ~10% broker economics\n> ${new Date().toLocaleDateString('en-US', { year: 'numeric', month: 'long', day: 'numeric' })}\n\n${raw}`; depthDocsGenerated++; setP('bank_funding', 'ok', 'BANK-FUNDING-PLAYBOOK.md ready'); } else { setP('bank_funding', 'sk', 'Insufficient content'); } } catch (e) { setP('bank_funding', 'sk', e.message); } } else { addP('bank_funding', 'Bank-ready funding playbook', 'Skipped (Demo / Express)'); setP('bank_funding', 'sk', 'Faster demo path'); } // ── HIGH-PRIORITY: Strategy + Accelerator + Financial (14/78) ──────────── // ── Group A — Strategy & Fundraising (4 docs) ──────────── const groupA = [ (async () => { addP('fundraising_strategy', 'Fundraising Strategy', '', '⬡ Phase 5c'); setP('fundraising_strategy', 'run'); try { const raw = await aiRaw(`You are a startup finance advisor. Generate a comprehensive FUNDRAISING STRATEGY for "${inf.name}" — ${desc}. Business type: ${inf.type_label}. Revenue model: ${inf.revenue_model}. Price point: ${inf.price}. Viability data: ${V2_SCORECARD} Cover ALL of the following with specific numbers and frameworks tailored to this business: 1. FUNDRAISING TIMING ANALYSIS: When to raise based on this business's metrics milestones (MRR thresholds, user counts, unit economics). Pre-seed vs seed vs bootstrap decision tree specific to ${inf.type_label} businesses. 2. CAPITAL REQUIREMENTS MODEL: Calculate 18-month runway needs. Break down: product development, go-to-market, operations, hiring. Provide low/mid/high scenarios with specific dollar amounts. 3. DEBT VS EQUITY FRAMEWORK: When revenue-based financing, venture debt, or SBA loans make sense for this revenue model (${inf.revenue_model}). Compare cost of capital across options. 4. DILUTION MODELING: Model founder ownership across Seed ($1-3M), Series A ($5-15M), Series B ($20-50M). Show dilution percentages at each stage assuming standard terms. 5. 83(b) ELECTION GUIDANCE: When to file, tax implications, step-by-step process, 30-day deadline warning, sample calculation showing tax savings. 6. SAFE VS PRICED ROUND DECISION FRAMEWORK: Decision matrix with criteria (amount raising, investor type, complexity tolerance, valuation confidence). Annotate when each instrument is optimal. 7. INVESTOR TARGETING CRITERIA: Build ideal investor profile for this business — check size range, sector focus, stage preference, value-add requirements. List 5 specific investor categories to target. 8. FUNDRAISING TIMELINE: Week-by-week 12-week fundraising sprint plan from deck prep through close. Use specific numbers, not ranges where possible. Reference the ${inf.price} price point and ${inf.revenue_model} model in financial projections.`, 5000); if (raw?.length > 500) { files['FUNDRAISING-STRATEGY.md'] = `# Fundraising Strategy — ${inf.name}\n\n${raw}`; setP('fundraising_strategy', 'ok', 'Done'); } else { setP('fundraising_strategy', 'sk', 'Skipped'); } } catch(e) { setP('fundraising_strategy', 'sk', e.message); } })(), (async () => { addP('org_design', 'Org Design', '', '⬡ Phase 5c'); setP('org_design', 'run'); try { const raw = await aiRaw(`You are an organizational design consultant. Generate a detailed ORG DESIGN document for "${inf.name}" — ${desc}. Business type: ${inf.type_label}. Revenue model: ${inf.revenue_model}. Viability data: ${V2_SCORECARD} Create a complete organizational blueprint covering: 1. ORGANIZATIONAL STRUCTURE: Design the org chart for 3 stages — founding team (1-5), growth stage (6-20), scale stage (21-50). Specify exact roles needed at each stage with hiring sequence priority. 2. RACI MATRIX: Build a RACI (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed) matrix for 12 key decisions: product roadmap, pricing changes, hiring, firing, budget allocation, partnerships, customer escalations, marketing campaigns, technical architecture, legal/compliance, fundraising, vendor selection. 3. REPORTING LINES: Define clear reporting structure. Who reports to whom at each stage. Identify where dual-reporting is acceptable vs harmful. 4. SPAN OF CONTROL: Recommend optimal span of control for each function (engineering: 5-7, sales: 8-10, support: 12-15). Justify based on ${inf.type_label} business complexity. 5. DECISION RIGHTS FRAMEWORK: Classify decisions as Type 1 (irreversible, CEO-only) vs Type 2 (reversible, delegate). Create a decision rights matrix with dollar thresholds and impact levels. 6. TEAM TOPOLOGY: Apply Team Topologies framework — identify stream-aligned teams, enabling teams, complicated-subsystem teams, and platform teams specific to building and scaling ${inf.name}. 7. FIRST 10 HIRES: Ordered list of first 10 hires with role title, key responsibilities, salary range, and why this hire at this position in the sequence. 8. CULTURE ARCHITECTURE: Define 5 operating principles, meeting cadence (daily/weekly/monthly/quarterly), communication norms, and remote vs hybrid vs in-person recommendation. Tailor everything to a ${inf.type_label} business with ${inf.revenue_model} revenue model.`, 5000); if (raw?.length > 500) { files['ORG-DESIGN.md'] = `# Organizational Design — ${inf.name}\n\n${raw}`; setP('org_design', 'ok', 'Done'); } else { setP('org_design', 'sk', 'Skipped'); } } catch(e) { setP('org_design', 'sk', e.message); } })(), (async () => { addP('ops_playbook', 'Operations Playbook', '', '⬡ Phase 5c'); setP('ops_playbook', 'run'); try { const raw = await aiRaw(`You are a COO and operations expert. Generate a comprehensive OPERATIONS PLAYBOOK for "${inf.name}" — ${desc}. Business type: ${inf.type_label}. Revenue model: ${inf.revenue_model}. Price: ${inf.price}. Viability data: ${V2_SCORECARD} Build a complete operations manual covering: 1. STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURES: Write SOP templates for the 8 most critical processes in a ${inf.type_label} business: customer onboarding, order fulfillment/service delivery, customer support escalation, billing and collections, vendor management, quality assurance, incident response, employee onboarding. Each SOP needs: purpose, scope, steps, responsible party, tools used, SLA. 2. CORE PROCESS FLOWS: Map the end-to-end process flow for the primary value delivery chain of ${inf.name}. From customer acquisition through delivery through renewal/repeat. Include decision points, handoffs, and cycle times. 3. CAPACITY PLANNING MODEL: Define capacity constraints for this business. How many customers can be served per employee/unit? What are the scaling triggers (at X customers, hire Y role)? Build a capacity planning table from 10 to 10,000 customers. 4. QUALITY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM: Define quality metrics, inspection points, acceptable defect rates, customer satisfaction targets (NPS/CSAT), and corrective action procedures. Specific to ${inf.type_label}. 5. KPI OWNERSHIP MATRIX: Assign every KPI to a specific role. Include: metric name, definition, target, measurement frequency, owner, escalation threshold. Cover financial, operational, customer, and product KPIs. 6. VENDOR & TOOL STACK: Recommended operational tools at each stage with monthly costs. Cover: project management, communication, documentation, monitoring, analytics. 7. DAILY/WEEKLY/MONTHLY OPERATING RHYTHM: Define the cadence of standups, reviews, retrospectives, planning sessions, and board updates. Make all SOPs actionable — someone should be able to follow them on day one.`, 5000); if (raw?.length > 500) { files['OPERATIONS-PLAYBOOK.md'] = `# Operations Playbook — ${inf.name}\n\n${raw}`; setP('ops_playbook', 'ok', 'Done'); } else { setP('ops_playbook', 'sk', 'Skipped'); } } catch(e) { setP('ops_playbook', 'sk', e.message); } })(), (async () => { addP('pivot_framework', 'Pivot Framework', '', '⬡ Phase 5c'); setP('pivot_framework', 'run'); try { const raw = await aiRaw(`You are a startup strategy advisor specializing in pivots. Generate a PIVOT FRAMEWORK for "${inf.name}" — ${desc}. Business type: ${inf.type_label}. Revenue model: ${inf.revenue_model}. Price: ${inf.price}. Viability data: ${V2_SCORECARD} Build a comprehensive pivot decision system: 1. PIVOT DECISION TRIGGERS: Define 10 quantitative triggers that signal a pivot is needed. For each: the metric, the threshold, the measurement period, and the confidence level required. Examples: "If CAC exceeds 3x LTV for 3 consecutive months" or "If Month-3 retention drops below 15%." Tailor to ${inf.revenue_model}. 2. PIVOT TYPE ANALYSIS: For ${inf.name} specifically, analyze 6 pivot types and rate feasibility (High/Medium/Low): - Zoom-in pivot (single feature becomes the product) - Zoom-out pivot (product becomes a feature of larger product) - Customer segment pivot (same product, different buyer) - Customer need pivot (same customer, different problem) - Channel pivot (same product, different distribution) - Revenue model pivot (same value, different monetization) 3. ADJACENT BUSINESS MODELS: Identify 5 specific adjacent business models ${inf.name} could pivot to. For each: describe the model, required changes, estimated time to pivot, capital required, and revenue potential. 4. STRATEGIC OPTIONALITY MAP: Create a 2x2 matrix of pivot options based on (market risk vs execution risk). Place each adjacent model on the matrix. 5. PIVOT EXECUTION PLAYBOOK: Step-by-step 8-week pivot execution plan: week 1-2 validation, week 3-4 MVP, week 5-6 testing, week 7-8 decision. Include stakeholder communication templates. 6. ANTI-PIVOT CHECKLIST: 5 reasons NOT to pivot (insufficient data, founder fatigue misread as market signal, etc.). Prevent premature pivoting. 7. CASE STUDIES: Reference 3 relevant pivot examples from companies in or adjacent to the ${inf.type_label} space. Be specific to this business — not generic startup advice.`, 5000); if (raw?.length > 500) { files['PIVOT-FRAMEWORK.md'] = `# Pivot Framework — ${inf.name}\n\n${raw}`; setP('pivot_framework', 'ok', 'Done'); } else { setP('pivot_framework', 'sk', 'Skipped'); } } catch(e) { setP('pivot_framework', 'sk', e.message); } })() ]; await Promise.allSettled(groupA); // ── Group B — Accelerator Essentials (4 docs) ──────────── const groupB = [ (async () => { addP('cohort_analysis', 'Cohort Analysis', '', '⬡ Phase 5c'); setP('cohort_analysis', 'run'); try { const raw = await aiRaw(`You are a growth analytics expert. Generate a COHORT ANALYSIS framework for "${inf.name}" — ${desc}. Business type: ${inf.type_label}. Revenue model: ${inf.revenue_model}. Price: ${inf.price}. Viability data: ${V2_SCORECARD} Build a complete cohort analysis system: 1. COHORT RETENTION CURVES: Design retention curve templates for ${inf.type_label} businesses. Define what "retained" means for this specific business (active usage? repeat purchase? subscription renewal?). Provide benchmark retention rates: Day 1, Day 7, Day 30, Month 2, Month 3, Month 6, Month 12. Show good/average/poor benchmarks for this business category. 2. COHORT REVENUE ANALYSIS: Template for tracking revenue per cohort over time. Include: initial ARPU, ARPU expansion rate, net revenue retention target, cohort LTV calculation methodology. Show how to calculate payback period per cohort. 3. BEHAVIORAL SEGMENTATION: Define 5 behavioral segments for ${inf.name} users based on engagement patterns. For each segment: name, defining behaviors, percentage of user base (expected), revenue contribution, recommended actions to move them to a higher-value segment. 4. MONTHLY COHORT TRACKING STRUCTURE: Design the exact spreadsheet structure — rows are cohort months, columns are periods since signup. Include formulas for: retention rate, cumulative revenue, average revenue per user, churn rate. Provide a 12x12 cohort table template. 5. INTERPRETATION GUIDE: How to read cohort charts. Common patterns and what they mean: smiling curves (good), frowning curves (bad), flat lines (concerning), hockey stick recovery (rare). What each pattern means for ${inf.name} specifically. 6. ACQUISITION CHANNEL COHORTS: How to segment cohorts by acquisition channel to identify highest-quality traffic sources. Define quality metrics beyond conversion: retention, LTV, referral rate. 7. COHORT-BASED FORECASTING: How to use cohort data to forecast revenue 6-12 months out. Provide the formula and methodology specific to ${inf.revenue_model}. Include specific numbers calibrated to the ${inf.price} price point.`, 5000); if (raw?.length > 500) { files['COHORT-ANALYSIS.md'] = `# Cohort Analysis Framework — ${inf.name}\n\n${raw}`; setP('cohort_analysis', 'ok', 'Done'); } else { setP('cohort_analysis', 'sk', 'Skipped'); } } catch(e) { setP('cohort_analysis', 'sk', e.message); } })(), (async () => { addP('retention_playbook', 'Retention Playbook', '', '⬡ Phase 5c'); setP('retention_playbook', 'run'); try { const raw = await aiRaw(`You are a retention and churn reduction specialist. Generate a RETENTION PLAYBOOK for "${inf.name}" — ${desc}. Business type: ${inf.type_label}. Revenue model: ${inf.revenue_model}. Price: ${inf.price}. Viability data: ${V2_SCORECARD} Build a comprehensive churn-fighting system: 1. CHURN SEGMENTATION: Break down churn into voluntary (active cancellation) vs involuntary (payment failure). For a ${inf.revenue_model} business at ${inf.price}, estimate the expected split. Define sub-categories: price-sensitive, feature-gap, competitor-switch, no-longer-need, poor-experience, payment-failure. 2. CHURN REASON TAXONOMY: Create a hierarchical churn reason classification with 20+ specific reasons organized under 5 categories. Include: the reason, frequency estimate, preventability score (1-5), and intervention cost. 3. SAVE FLOW SCRIPTS: Write 5 complete cancellation save flow scripts for the top 5 churn reasons. Each script: opening acknowledgment, probing question, tailored offer (discount/pause/downgrade/feature unlock), close. Include specific dollar amounts based on ${inf.price}. 4. REACTIVATION CAMPAIGNS: Design a 3-email reactivation sequence for churned customers. Include: timing (days post-churn), subject lines, body copy, offer strategy, segmentation by churn reason. Write the actual email copy. 5. RETENTION METRICS DASHBOARD: Define the 12 metrics that belong on a retention dashboard. For each: metric name, formula, target for ${inf.type_label} businesses, measurement frequency, alert threshold. 6. WIN-BACK EMAIL SEQUENCE: Write a 5-email win-back sequence sent over 90 days to churned users. Email 1 (Day 3): "We miss you." Email 2 (Day 14): Value reminder. Email 3 (Day 30): New feature announcement. Email 4 (Day 60): Special offer. Email 5 (Day 90): Final attempt. Write full copy for each. 7. INVOLUNTARY CHURN PREVENTION: Smart dunning sequence — retry schedule, pre-dunning notifications, payment update flow, grace period strategy. Expected recovery rate for each tactic. 8. LEADING INDICATORS: Identify 8 behavioral signals that predict churn 30-60 days before it happens for ${inf.name}. For each: the signal, detection method, intervention playbook.`, 5000); if (raw?.length > 500) { files['RETENTION-PLAYBOOK.md'] = `# Retention Playbook — ${inf.name}\n\n${raw}`; setP('retention_playbook', 'ok', 'Done'); } else { setP('retention_playbook', 'sk', 'Skipped'); } } catch(e) { setP('retention_playbook', 'sk', e.message); } })(), (async () => { addP('cap_table', 'Cap Table Model', '', '⬡ Phase 5c'); setP('cap_table', 'run'); try { const raw = await aiRaw(`You are a startup attorney and cap table expert. Generate a CAP TABLE MODEL for "${inf.name}" — ${desc}. Business type: ${inf.type_label}. Revenue model: ${inf.revenue_model}. Price: ${inf.price}. Viability data: ${V2_SCORECARD} Build a complete cap table guide and model: 1. FOUNDER SPLIT FRAMEWORK: Decision framework for 1-4 co-founders. Factors: idea origination (5-10%), domain expertise (10-20%), time commitment (20-30%), capital contribution (10-20%), CEO role premium (5-10%). Provide 3 example splits for common founder configurations. Include the "equal split" argument and when it's actually optimal. 2. ESOP POOL SIZING: Recommend ESOP pool size (typically 10-20%) based on stage and hiring plan. Show how pool creation dilutes founders. Option pool shuffle explanation. Provide a sample option grant table for first 10 employees with percentage ranges by role. 3. DILUTION MODELING ACROSS ROUNDS: - Pre-seed: $500K on $5M cap SAFE → show resulting ownership - Seed: $2M on $10M pre-money → show resulting ownership - Series A: $8M on $30M pre-money → show resulting ownership - Series B: $25M on $100M pre-money → show resulting ownership Show founder ownership percentage at each stage with and without pro-rata participation. 4. PRO-RATA RIGHTS: Explain pro-rata rights, super pro-rata, and pay-to-play provisions. When to grant them, when to fight for them as founder. Dollar impact at each round. 5. VESTING SCHEDULES: Standard 4-year/1-year cliff with monthly vesting thereafter. Explain: single-trigger vs double-trigger acceleration, good leaver vs bad leaver provisions, reverse vesting for founders. Provide sample vesting schedule table. 6. ACCELERATION CLAUSES: Single-trigger (change of control) vs double-trigger (change of control + termination). Recommend which is appropriate for each role level. 7. 409A VALUATION: When it's needed, how it works, typical cost, how it sets strike price. Rule of thumb: 409A is typically 25-30% of preferred price. Explain safe harbor provisions. 8. CAP TABLE TEMPLATE: Provide a text-based cap table showing columns: shareholder, share class, shares, percentage, invested, price/share. Populate with realistic numbers for ${inf.name}.`, 5000); if (raw?.length > 500) { files['CAP-TABLE-MODEL.md'] = `# Cap Table Model — ${inf.name}\n\n${raw}`; setP('cap_table', 'ok', 'Done'); } else { setP('cap_table', 'sk', 'Skipped'); } } catch(e) { setP('cap_table', 'sk', e.message); } })(), (async () => { addP('term_sheets', 'Term Sheet Templates', '', '⬡ Phase 5c'); setP('term_sheets', 'run'); try { const raw = await aiRaw(`You are a venture capital attorney. Generate TERM SHEET TEMPLATES and guidance for "${inf.name}" — ${desc}. Business type: ${inf.type_label}. Revenue model: ${inf.revenue_model}. Price: ${inf.price}. Viability data: ${V2_SCORECARD} Create comprehensive fundraising document templates: 1. SAFE TEMPLATE WITH ANNOTATIONS: Write a complete YC-style SAFE (Simple Agreement for Future Equity) with inline annotations explaining each clause. Cover: valuation cap, discount rate, MFN (most favored nation), pro-rata rights, dissolution preference. Use brackets for customizable values. Annotate which terms are founder-friendly vs investor-friendly. 2. CONVERTIBLE NOTE TEMPLATE: Write a convertible note template with annotations. Cover: principal amount, interest rate (typically 2-8%), maturity date (18-24 months), conversion mechanics, qualified financing threshold, valuation cap, discount. Explain how conversion math works with a numerical example. 3. VALUATION CAP EXPLANATION: Deep dive on valuation caps. How they work mechanically — show with exact math: if cap is $8M and Series A is at $20M pre-money, what percentage does SAFE holder get? Include both pre-money and post-money SAFE mechanics. 4. DISCOUNT RATE ANALYSIS: Standard discount rates (15-25%). Show conversion math at 20% discount. When to negotiate higher vs lower discounts. Interaction between cap and discount (which applies? — typically more favorable to investor). 5. MFN CLAUSE DEEP DIVE: What Most Favored Nation means in practice. When it triggers. Why later SAFEs can be dangerous for earlier investors without MFN. Strategic implications for founders issuing multiple SAFEs. 6. PRO-RATA RIGHTS IN SAFES: Side letter template for pro-rata rights. When investors can participate in future rounds. Major vs minor investor thresholds. 7. KEY TERMS GLOSSARY: Define 25 essential fundraising terms: liquidation preference, anti-dilution (broad vs narrow weighted average), drag-along, tag-along, information rights, board composition, protective provisions, no-shop, right of first refusal, co-sale rights, redemption rights, dividends, registration rights, founders' activities, non-compete, IP assignment, representations and warranties, indemnification, governing law, pay-to-play, participation rights, conversion rights, voting rights, preemptive rights, observer rights. Tailor valuation ranges and amounts to what's realistic for a ${inf.type_label} startup.`, 5000); if (raw?.length > 500) { files['TERM-SHEET-TEMPLATES.md'] = `# Term Sheet Templates — ${inf.name}\n\n${raw}`; setP('term_sheets', 'ok', 'Done'); } else { setP('term_sheets', 'sk', 'Skipped'); } } catch(e) { setP('term_sheets', 'sk', e.message); } })() ]; await Promise.allSettled(groupB); // ── Group C — Serial Entrepreneur (4 docs) ──────────── const groupC = [ (async () => { addP('crisis_playbook', 'Crisis Playbook', '', '⬡ Phase 5c'); setP('crisis_playbook', 'run'); try { const raw = await aiRaw(`You are a startup crisis management advisor. Generate a CRISIS PLAYBOOK for "${inf.name}" — ${desc}. Business type: ${inf.type_label}. Revenue model: ${inf.revenue_model}. Viability data: ${V2_SCORECARD} Build a comprehensive crisis response system with detailed playbooks for each of these 10 crisis scenarios: 1. CO-FOUNDER CONFLICT: Equity disputes, vision disagreements, effort imbalance. Detection signs, mediation steps, buyout mechanics, operating agreement enforcement, legal nuclear options. 2. KEY EMPLOYEE DEPARTURE: CTO/lead engineer/top salesperson leaves. Knowledge transfer emergency protocol, counter-offer decision framework, 30-day transition plan, competitive threat assessment. 3. CUSTOMER CONCENTRATION RISK: When >25% revenue comes from one client. Diversification timeline, relationship deepening vs dependency reduction, contract renegotiation for protection. 4. CASH CRUNCH: <3 months runway remaining. 48-hour triage plan: cut list priority order, revenue acceleration tactics, emergency bridge financing options, vendor negotiation scripts for payment extensions. 5. PR DISASTER: Negative press, viral social media backlash, customer data complaint goes public. First-hour response protocol, spokesperson designation, statement templates, social media response framework. 6. LEGAL THREAT: Cease & desist, patent troll, employee lawsuit, regulatory inquiry. First 24 hours: what to do and NOT do. When to engage counsel. Insurance (D&O, E&O) activation. Cost estimation. 7. DATA BREACH: Customer data compromised. Incident response timeline (first 1/4/24/72 hours). Notification requirements by jurisdiction. Customer communication templates. Forensics and remediation steps. 8. COMPETITOR UNDERCUT: Competitor launches similar product at 50% of your price. Response framework: when to match, when to differentiate, when to ignore. Value communication pivots. 9. REGULATORY CHANGE: New regulation impacts ${inf.type_label} business model. Compliance assessment framework, lobbying options, business model adaptation, timeline management. 10. MARKET CRASH/RECESSION: Economic downturn playbook. Cash preservation mode, pricing adjustments, customer retention focus, counter-cyclical opportunities, hiring from distressed competitors. For each scenario include: early warning signs, severity assessment (1-5), immediate actions (first 24 hours), 30-day resolution plan, and estimated cost impact.`, 5000); if (raw?.length > 500) { files['CRISIS-PLAYBOOK.md'] = `# Crisis Playbook — ${inf.name}\n\n${raw}`; setP('crisis_playbook', 'ok', 'Done'); } else { setP('crisis_playbook', 'sk', 'Skipped'); } } catch(e) { setP('crisis_playbook', 'sk', e.message); } })(), (async () => { addP('experiment_framework', 'Experiment Framework', '', '⬡ Phase 5c'); setP('experiment_framework', 'run'); try { const raw = await aiRaw(`You are a growth experimentation expert (Lean Startup methodology). Generate an EXPERIMENT FRAMEWORK for "${inf.name}" — ${desc}. Business type: ${inf.type_label}. Revenue model: ${inf.revenue_model}. Price: ${inf.price}. Viability data: ${V2_SCORECARD} Build a complete experimentation system: 1. BUILD-MEASURE-LEARN LOOP: Define the exact Build-Measure-Learn loop for ${inf.name}. What constitutes a "build" (minimum viable test)? What do you "measure" (specific metrics)? What do you "learn" (decision criteria)? Define cycle time target (ideally 1-2 weeks). 2. HYPOTHESIS TEMPLATE: Provide a fill-in-the-blank hypothesis template: "We believe that [change] for [segment] will cause [metric] to [direction] by [amount] because [rationale]. We will measure this by [method] over [timeframe]. Success criteria: [threshold]. If wrong, we will [next action]." Provide 5 completed examples specific to ${inf.name}. 3. EXPERIMENT DESIGN GUIDE: Types of experiments ranked by cost and speed: landing page smoke tests, fake door tests, concierge MVP, Wizard of Oz, A/B tests, multivariate tests, holdout groups. When to use each for ${inf.type_label} businesses. 4. MINIMUM SAMPLE SIZES: Statistical significance calculator reference. For ${inf.price} price point and expected conversion rates, calculate minimum sample sizes for detecting 10%, 20%, and 50% lifts at 95% confidence and 80% power. Include runtime estimates based on expected traffic. 5. EXPERIMENT BACKLOG TEMPLATE: Create a prioritized backlog of 15 experiments for ${inf.name} using ICE scoring (Impact 1-10, Confidence 1-10, Ease 1-10). Include: experiment name, hypothesis, ICE score, estimated duration, resources needed, success metric. 6. INNOVATION ACCOUNTING: Define the 3 levels: (a) establish baseline metrics, (b) tune the engine toward ideal, (c) pivot or persevere decision. Create the specific dashboard for ${inf.name} with baseline → target for each metric. 7. EXPERIMENT DOCUMENTATION: Template for documenting completed experiments: hypothesis, design, duration, sample size, results, statistical significance, decision made, learnings, follow-up experiments.`, 5000); if (raw?.length > 500) { files['EXPERIMENT-FRAMEWORK.md'] = `# Experiment Framework — ${inf.name}\n\n${raw}`; setP('experiment_framework', 'ok', 'Done'); } else { setP('experiment_framework', 'sk', 'Skipped'); } } catch(e) { setP('experiment_framework', 'sk', e.message); } })(), (async () => { addP('growth_experiments', 'Growth Experiments', '', '⬡ Phase 5c'); setP('growth_experiments', 'run'); try { const raw = await aiRaw(`You are a growth hacker and AARRR funnel expert. Generate 20 GROWTH EXPERIMENTS for "${inf.name}" — ${desc}. Business type: ${inf.type_label}. Revenue model: ${inf.revenue_model}. Price: ${inf.price}. Viability data: ${V2_SCORECARD} Organize 20 specific, actionable growth experiments by pirate metrics funnel stage: AWARENESS (4 experiments): - Design 4 experiments to increase brand awareness and top-of-funnel traffic for ${inf.name}. Include: content marketing hack, partnership/co-marketing play, community-driven growth tactic, unconventional PR stunt. For each: hypothesis, implementation steps, expected cost, expected timeline, success metric and target. ACQUISITION (4 experiments): - Design 4 experiments to convert visitors to signups/leads. Include: landing page optimization, lead magnet creation, channel arbitrage opportunity, paid acquisition creative test. Specific to ${inf.type_label} with ${inf.price} price point. Calculate expected CAC for each. ACTIVATION (3 experiments): - Design 3 experiments to improve first-use experience and "aha moment" achievement. Define what the aha moment is for ${inf.name}. Include: onboarding flow optimization, time-to-value reduction, activation email sequence. RETENTION (3 experiments): - Design 3 experiments to improve retention for a ${inf.revenue_model} business. Include: engagement loop creation, habit formation trigger, re-engagement campaign. Define retention period and target rates. REFERRAL (3 experiments): - Design 3 referral/viral loop experiments. Include: referral program mechanics (give $X get $X), viral loop within the product, social proof/UGC campaign. Calculate viral coefficient targets. REVENUE (3 experiments): - Design 3 revenue optimization experiments. Include: pricing page A/B test, upsell/cross-sell flow, annual plan incentive. Calculate expected revenue impact at ${inf.price} price point. VIRAL LOOP DESIGN: Design a complete viral loop specific to ${inf.name}: trigger → share → accept → value → trigger. Calculate target viral coefficient (K>1 for organic growth). REFERRAL PROGRAM TEMPLATE: Complete referral program design: incentive structure (both sides), sharing mechanics, tracking, fraud prevention, terms. GROWTH MODEL: Build a simple growth model showing how these experiments compound. Monthly growth rate targets for months 1-12.`, 5000); if (raw?.length > 500) { files['GROWTH-EXPERIMENTS.md'] = `# Growth Experiments — ${inf.name}\n\n${raw}`; setP('growth_experiments', 'ok', 'Done'); } else { setP('growth_experiments', 'sk', 'Skipped'); } } catch(e) { setP('growth_experiments', 'sk', e.message); } })(), (async () => { addP('customer_dev', 'Customer Development', '', '⬡ Phase 5c'); setP('customer_dev', 'run'); try { const raw = await aiRaw(`You are a customer development expert (Steve Blank methodology). Generate a CUSTOMER DEVELOPMENT guide for "${inf.name}" — ${desc}. Business type: ${inf.type_label}. Revenue model: ${inf.revenue_model}. Price: ${inf.price}. Viability data: ${V2_SCORECARD} Build a complete customer development playbook using Blank's 4-step model: STEP 1 — CUSTOMER DISCOVERY: - Define 3 target customer personas for ${inf.name} with demographics, psychographics, jobs-to-be-done, pains, and gains. - Write 15 customer interview questions (open-ended, non-leading) to validate problem-solution fit. Avoid "would you use" questions — focus on current behavior and pain. - Design a problem validation experiment: how to test if the problem is real and worth solving with <$500 and <2 weeks. - Hypothesis table: 10 testable hypotheses about the customer, problem, solution, channel, and revenue model. STEP 2 — CUSTOMER VALIDATION: - Define the minimum criteria for "product-market fit" for ${inf.name}: Sean Ellis survey target (>40% "very disappointed"), NPS target, retention rate, organic growth signals. - Design a sales validation experiment: can you sell ${inf.name} at ${inf.price} before the product is fully built? Concierge/Wizard of Oz approach. - Conversion funnel benchmarks: visit→signup, signup→activation, activation→payment, payment→retention targets. STEP 3 — CUSTOMER CREATION: - Demand generation strategy for ${inf.type_label} businesses. Which channels to test first (pick 3 from: SEO, SEM, content, social, partnerships, cold outreach, community, events). - Channel testing methodology: how to run a $500 test per channel and determine winner. Metrics to track, timeline, decision criteria. - Positioning statement template filled in for ${inf.name}. STEP 4 — COMPANY BUILDING: - Transition triggers: what metrics indicate it's time to shift from search mode to execution mode. - Scaling playbook: processes to formalize, roles to hire, tools to implement. - The "pivot triggers" — specific metric thresholds that indicate the current approach isn't working. INTERVIEW LOGISTICS: Where to find interviewees (5 specific sources for ${inf.type_label} customers), how to recruit them, compensation, interview duration and format, note-taking template, synthesis framework.`, 5000); if (raw?.length > 500) { files['CUSTOMER-DEVELOPMENT.md'] = `# Customer Development — ${inf.name}\n\n${raw}`; setP('customer_dev', 'ok', 'Done'); } else { setP('customer_dev', 'sk', 'Skipped'); } } catch(e) { setP('customer_dev', 'sk', e.message); } })() ]; await Promise.allSettled(groupC); // ── Group D — Financial (3 docs) ──────────── const groupD = [ (async () => { addP('tax_strategy', 'Tax Strategy', '', '⬡ Phase 5c'); setP('tax_strategy', 'run'); try { const raw = await aiRaw(`You are a startup tax strategist and CPA. Generate a TAX STRATEGY guide for "${inf.name}" — ${desc}. Business type: ${inf.type_label}. Revenue model: ${inf.revenue_model}. Price: ${inf.price}. Viability data: ${V2_SCORECARD} Build a comprehensive tax optimization strategy: 1. R&D TAX CREDITS: Qualification criteria for ${inf.type_label} businesses — which activities count (software development, UX research, algorithm design, cloud architecture). Calculate potential credit: if $200K in qualifying expenses, federal credit = $13K-$20K (6.5-10%). State R&D credits by top 5 startup states. Documentation requirements: contemporaneous logs, project tracking, employee time allocation. How startups <$5M revenue can apply credit against payroll taxes (up to $250K/year for 5 years). 2. QSBS SECTION 1202: Qualified Small Business Stock exclusion — potentially exclude $10M or 10x basis from capital gains. Qualification requirements: C-corp, original issuance, held 5+ years, active business (not consulting/professional services — assess if ${inf.type_label} qualifies). Planning strategies to maximize QSBS benefit. State-level QSBS treatment (CA doesn't conform). 3. STATE TAX OPTIMIZATION: Compare incorporation in DE (franchise tax), WY (no state tax), NV (no state tax) vs operating state obligations. Nexus rules for remote teams. Sales tax obligations for ${inf.revenue_model} businesses (SaaS tax by state). State income tax planning for founders. 4. ENTITY-SPECIFIC STRATEGIES: Compare LLC (pass-through), S-Corp (salary + distribution to save FICA), C-Corp (21% flat rate, retained earnings, QSBS eligible). Recommendation for ${inf.name} at each revenue stage: <$100K, $100K-$500K, $500K-$1M, $1M+. 5. ESTIMATED QUARTERLY TAXES: When to start paying (expect to owe >$1K), safe harbor rules (pay 100% of prior year or 90% of current year), penalty avoidance, payment schedule (Apr 15, Jun 15, Sep 15, Jan 15). 6. DEDUCTION MAXIMIZATION: Startup costs deduction ($5K first year, amortize rest over 180 months), Section 179 expensing, home office deduction, health insurance deduction (S-Corp owners), retirement plan options (SEP-IRA, Solo 401k), vehicle deductions if applicable. 7. ROBS (ROLLOVER AS BUSINESS STARTUP): High-level mechanics — new C corp, new qualified plan, rollover of eligible retirement funds, plan purchases employer stock to fund the company. Contrast with informal "LLC pyramid" strategies. Cover: why marketers say "C corp + retirement" vs double-taxation fear; accurate treatment of corporate tax, W-2 income, and tax-deferred growth inside the plan; prohibited transactions; annual compliance; exit/sale when plan owns equity. State clearly that ROBS is not a generic substitute for entity choice — it is a regulated capitalization path. Cross-reference ENTITY-SELECTION.md. Mandatory: engage a ROBS-compliant third-party administrator and ERISA-savvy counsel. 8. WHEN TO HIRE A CPA: Revenue thresholds, complexity triggers, expected costs ($2K-$10K/year), what to look for in a startup CPA, questions to ask. DIY tools for early stage (Bench, Pilot, Fondo). Include specific dollar amounts and calculations where possible.`, 5000); if (raw?.length > 500) { files['TAX-STRATEGY.md'] = `# Tax Strategy — ${inf.name}\n\n${raw}`; setP('tax_strategy', 'ok', 'Done'); } else { setP('tax_strategy', 'sk', 'Skipped'); } } catch(e) { setP('tax_strategy', 'sk', e.message); } })(), (async () => { addP('pricing_strategy', 'Pricing Strategy', '', '⬡ Phase 5c'); setP('pricing_strategy', 'run'); try { const raw = await aiRaw(`You are a pricing strategy consultant. Generate a PRICING STRATEGY for "${inf.name}" — ${desc}. Business type: ${inf.type_label}. Revenue model: ${inf.revenue_model}. Current price point: ${inf.price}. Viability data: ${V2_SCORECARD} Build a comprehensive pricing optimization system: 1. PRICING PSYCHOLOGY TACTICS: Apply these principles to ${inf.name}: - Anchoring: Design a 3-tier pricing page where the middle tier is the target. Set anchor prices. Show the exact layout. - Decoy effect: Design a decoy option that makes the target tier look like the best value. Explain the math. - Charm pricing: When to use $X9 vs $X7 vs round numbers. Recommendation for ${inf.price} price range. - Price framing: "Only $X/day" or "$X/month" or "$X/year (save 20%)". Calculate all frames for ${inf.price}. - Loss aversion: Frame pricing around what customers lose by NOT buying. 2. VALUE-BASED PRICING METHODOLOGY: Calculate the value ${inf.name} creates for customers. Quantify: time saved, money saved, revenue generated, risk reduced. Price at 10-20% of value created. Show the calculation. 3. COMPETITIVE PRICING ANALYSIS: Framework for analyzing competitor pricing in the ${inf.type_label} space. Create a comparison matrix template. Positioning strategies: premium, value, penetration, freemium. Recommend one for ${inf.name} with justification. 4. FREEMIUM CONVERSION OPTIMIZATION (if applicable to ${inf.revenue_model}): Define the free tier boundary — enough value to hook, clear upgrade trigger. Target 2-5% free-to-paid conversion. Design the upgrade moment. Map the 5 "aha moments" that trigger upgrade intent. 5. PRICE TESTING FRAMEWORK: A/B testing pricing without destroying trust. Methods: geographic testing, cohort testing, new-visitor-only testing, feature-based testing. Statistical requirements for pricing tests. How to handle price changes for existing customers. 6. WILLINGNESS-TO-PAY RESEARCH: Van Westendorp Price Sensitivity Meter — the 4 questions, how to run the survey, how to analyze results, how to find the optimal price point. Include sample survey design for ${inf.name}. Gabor-Granger methodology as alternative. 7. PRICING PAGE DESIGN: Best practices for the pricing page. Element checklist: social proof placement, FAQ, feature comparison table, money-back guarantee, annual vs monthly toggle, enterprise CTA. Wireframe description for ${inf.name}'s pricing page. 8. MONETIZATION LEVERS BEYOND PRICE: Usage-based add-ons, premium support tiers, API access, white-label licensing, data/insights products, marketplace commissions. Identify 3 secondary revenue streams for ${inf.name}.`, 5000); if (raw?.length > 500) { files['PRICING-STRATEGY.md'] = `# Pricing Strategy — ${inf.name}\n\n${raw}`; setP('pricing_strategy', 'ok', 'Done'); } else { setP('pricing_strategy', 'sk', 'Skipped'); } } catch(e) { setP('pricing_strategy', 'sk', e.message); } })(), (async () => { addP('robs_checklist', 'ROBS checklist', '', '\u2b21 Phase 5c'); setP('robs_checklist', 'run'); try { const raw = await aiRaw(`You are an ERISA-aware business attorney working with a ROBS third-party administrator (TPA). Produce a single founder-facing checklist for "${inf.name}" (${inf.type_label}, ${inf.revenue_model}). Output markdown for file docs/ROBS-CHECKLIST.md with these sections only (use ## headings): 1. **What ROBS is (one paragraph)** — C corporation + new qualified plan + rollover + plan purchases employer stock; not an "LLC pyramid." 2. **Before you start (go / no-go)** — minimum rollover size worth admin cost, appetite for corporate formalities, need for W-2 reasonable compensation if you are an employee. 3. **Questions for your TPA / counsel (numbered)** — at least 12 specific questions (FMV of stock, prohibited transactions, unrelated business income nuances at high level, Form 5500, fidelity bond, plan document, stock valuation cadence, exit if company is sold, diversification rules, corrective actions if misstep). 4. **Implementation sequence (checkboxes)** — ordered steps from entity formation through first payroll if applicable. 5. **Ongoing compliance calendar** — annual and quarterly reminders (5500, plan review, corporate minutes, compensation review). 6. **Red flags** — informal loans between plan and company, personal use of plan assets, ignoring FMV, mixing personal expenses, skipping documentation. 7. **Related docs** — point readers to ENTITY-SELECTION.md and TAX-STRATEGY.md in this bundle. End with a bold disclaimer: not legal or tax advice; ROBS requires a specialized administrator and counsel. Keep total length under ~900 words; dense bullets OK.`, 3500); if (raw?.length > 400) { files['docs/ROBS-CHECKLIST.md'] = `# ROBS checklist — ${inf.name}\n\n${raw}`; setP('robs_checklist', 'ok', 'Done'); } else { setP('robs_checklist', 'sk', 'Skipped'); } } catch(e) { setP('robs_checklist', 'sk', e.message); } })() ]; await Promise.allSettled(groupD); // ── END: Strategy + Accelerator + Financial docs ──────────── // ── HIGH-PRIORITY: Legal + GTM + People + Product (14/78) ──────────── // ── Group E — Legal (4 docs) ──────────── addP('nda', 'NDA Template', '', '⬡ Legal Docs'); setP('nda', 'run'); addP('contractor', 'Contractor Agreement', '', '⬡ Legal Docs'); setP('contractor', 'run'); addP('offer', 'Offer Letter Template', '', '⬡ Legal Docs'); setP('offer', 'run'); addP('gdpr', 'GDPR Implementation', '', '⬡ Legal Docs'); setP('gdpr', 'run'); const [ndaR, contractorR, offerR, gdprR] = await Promise.allSettled([ (async () => { try { const raw = await aiRaw(`Generate a comprehensive Mutual NDA template for "${inf.name}" (${inf.type_label}). ${desc} Include BOTH a mutual and unilateral version. Cover: 1. DEFINITION OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION — broad definition covering business plans, financials, customer data, technical specs, trade secrets, algorithms, source code, marketing strategies. Clearly enumerate categories. 2. EXCLUSIONS — publicly available info, independently developed, received from third party without restriction, required by law/court order (with notice obligation). 3. OBLIGATIONS OF RECEIVING PARTY — use reasonable care (no less than own confidential info), limit access to need-to-know, no reverse engineering, return/destroy on termination, no copying except as necessary. 4. TERM — 2-year confidentiality period from date of disclosure, survival clause for trade secrets (indefinite). 5. REMEDIES — acknowledge irreparable harm, injunctive relief without bond, indemnification for breach, attorney fees to prevailing party. 6. JURISDICTION — governing law clause with state placeholder, dispute resolution (mediation then arbitration), venue selection. 7. MUTUAL VERSION — symmetric obligations, both parties as Discloser and Receiver. 8. UNILATERAL VERSION — one-way protection, clearly labeled alternative clauses. 9. SIGNATURE BLOCKS — company name, authorized representative, title, date, address, witness line. 10. SCHEDULES — Schedule A (description of contemplated transaction), Schedule B (specific exclusions). Format as a ready-to-use legal template with [BRACKETED PLACEHOLDERS] for customization. Include instruction notes in italics.`, 4000); if (raw?.length > 500) { files['docs/NDA-TEMPLATE.md'] = `# Mutual Non-Disclosure Agreement — ${inf.name}\n\n${raw}`; setP('nda', 'ok', 'Done'); } else { setP('nda', 'sk', 'Skipped'); } } catch(e) { setP('nda', 'sk', e.message); } })(), (async () => { try { const raw = await aiRaw(`Generate a comprehensive Independent Contractor Agreement template for "${inf.name}" (${inf.type_label}). ${desc} Cover ALL of the following in detail: 1. SCOPE OF WORK TEMPLATE — deliverables table (description, due date, acceptance criteria), change order process, statement of work exhibit format. 2. PAYMENT TERMS — rate/fee structure (hourly, fixed, milestone), invoicing schedule (net-30), late payment penalties (1.5%/month), expense reimbursement policy, payment method. 3. IP ASSIGNMENT CLAUSE — all work product assigned to company, work-for-hire designation, moral rights waiver, pre-existing IP carve-out with schedule, license to pre-existing IP incorporated in deliverables. 4. TERMINATION — termination for convenience (30-day notice), termination for cause (10-day cure), effect on payment (pay for completed work), wind-down obligations, survival clauses. 5. NON-COMPETE — limited scope (direct competitors only), reasonable duration (12 months), geographic limitation, carve-out for pre-existing clients. 6. CONFIDENTIALITY — definition, obligations (mirror NDA terms), return of materials, survival (2 years post-termination). 7. INDEMNIFICATION — mutual indemnification, contractor indemnifies for negligence/IP infringement, limitation of liability (cap at fees paid). 8. INSURANCE — contractor to maintain general liability ($1M), professional liability/E&O ($1M), workers comp if applicable, proof of insurance requirement. 9. IRS 20-FACTOR WORKER CLASSIFICATION TEST — behavioral control analysis, financial control analysis, relationship type analysis, safe harbor provisions, Form W-9 requirement, no benefits/withholding confirmation. 10. REPRESENTATIONS & WARRANTIES — authority to enter agreement, no conflicts, compliance with laws, original work. 11. DISPUTE RESOLUTION — mediation, then binding arbitration, governing law, venue. Format as a ready-to-use legal template with [BRACKETED PLACEHOLDERS].`, 4000); if (raw?.length > 500) { files['docs/CONTRACTOR-AGREEMENT.md'] = `# Independent Contractor Agreement — ${inf.name}\n\n${raw}`; setP('contractor', 'ok', 'Done'); } else { setP('contractor', 'sk', 'Skipped'); } } catch(e) { setP('contractor', 'sk', e.message); } })(), (async () => { try { const raw = await aiRaw(`Generate a comprehensive Employee Offer Letter template for "${inf.name}" (${inf.type_label}). ${desc} The business uses a ${inf.revenue_model} revenue model at ${inf.price} price point. Cover: 1. POSITION DETAILS — job title, department, reporting manager, start date, employment type (full-time/part-time), work location (remote/hybrid/onsite), FLSA classification (exempt/non-exempt). 2. COMPENSATION — base salary (annual and per-pay-period), pay frequency (bi-weekly/semi-monthly), bonus structure (target % and criteria), commission plan if applicable, signing bonus with clawback terms. 3. EQUITY — stock option grant (number of shares), vesting schedule (4-year with 1-year cliff), exercise price (FMV at grant), option type (ISO vs NSO), reference to full Stock Option Agreement. 4. BENEFITS SUMMARY — health insurance (medical/dental/vision with employer contribution %), 401(k) with match details, PTO policy (days per year), sick leave, parental leave, life/disability insurance, professional development budget, equipment/stipend. 5. AT-WILL EMPLOYMENT — clear at-will statement, either party can terminate with or without cause, no guaranteed duration. 6. NON-COMPETE/NON-SOLICIT — reasonable scope and duration, non-solicitation of employees (12 months), non-solicitation of clients (12 months), non-compete if applicable (with geographic/industry limits). 7. IP ASSIGNMENT REFERENCE — reference to PIIA/CIIAA to be signed, all work product belongs to company, prior inventions disclosure schedule. 8. CONDITIONS OF EMPLOYMENT — background check, right to work verification (I-9), reference check, signing of additional agreements (PIIA, NDA, employee handbook acknowledgment), drug screening if applicable. 9. CONFIDENTIALITY — obligation to protect company information, reference to NDA. 10. ACCEPTANCE — signature block, expiration date for offer (7-14 days), counter-signature by company. Format as a professional offer letter template with [BRACKETED PLACEHOLDERS]. Include warm, welcoming tone appropriate for a startup.`, 4000); if (raw?.length > 500) { files['docs/OFFER-LETTER-TEMPLATE.md'] = `# Employee Offer Letter Template — ${inf.name}\n\n${raw}`; setP('offer', 'ok', 'Done'); } else { setP('offer', 'sk', 'Skipped'); } } catch(e) { setP('offer', 'sk', e.message); } })(), (async () => { try { const raw = await aiRaw(`Generate a comprehensive GDPR/CCPA Implementation Guide for "${inf.name}" (${inf.type_label}). ${desc} This is a ${inf.revenue_model} business at ${inf.price}. Cover ALL of the following in actionable detail: 1. COOKIE CONSENT IMPLEMENTATION — cookie categories (strictly necessary, functional, analytics, marketing), consent banner requirements (GDPR vs CCPA differences), implementation code snippets for consent management, cookie audit template (name, provider, purpose, duration, type), Google Consent Mode v2 integration. 2. DATA SUBJECT RIGHTS WORKFLOW — right of access (SAR process, 30-day timeline), right to erasure (deletion checklist across all systems), right to rectification, right to portability (data export format), right to restrict processing, right to object, automated decision-making opt-out. Include workflow diagrams and response letter templates for each right. 3. DATA PROCESSING INVENTORY — template for recording all processing activities (Article 30), data flow mapping methodology, categories of data subjects, categories of personal data, purposes and legal bases, retention periods, third-party processors list, international transfers. 4. DATA PROCESSING AGREEMENT (DPA) TEMPLATE — sub-processor obligations, security measures (Article 32), breach notification obligations, audit rights, data return/deletion on termination, standard contractual clauses reference. 5. BREACH NOTIFICATION PROCEDURE — detection and classification (severity matrix), 72-hour supervisory authority notification (template), individual notification threshold and template, breach register template, post-incident review process, documentation requirements. 6. PRIVACY IMPACT ASSESSMENT (PIA/DPIA) TEMPLATE — when DPIA is required (high-risk processing), systematic description of processing, necessity and proportionality assessment, risk identification and scoring matrix, mitigation measures, consultation triggers, sign-off process. 7. LAWFUL BASIS DETERMINATION — decision tree for selecting lawful basis (consent, contract, legitimate interest, legal obligation, vital interest, public task), Legitimate Interest Assessment (LIA) template, consent requirements (freely given, specific, informed, unambiguous), records of consent. 8. CCPA-SPECIFIC REQUIREMENTS — "Do Not Sell My Personal Information" link, categories of PI collected/sold/disclosed, financial incentive notices, service provider contracts, consumer request verification, 12-month lookback requirement. Include implementation timelines, responsible parties, and compliance checklists.`, 5000); if (raw?.length > 500) { files['docs/GDPR-IMPLEMENTATION.md'] = `# GDPR/CCPA Implementation Guide — ${inf.name}\n\n${raw}`; setP('gdpr', 'ok', 'Done'); } else { setP('gdpr', 'sk', 'Skipped'); } } catch(e) { setP('gdpr', 'sk', e.message); } })() ]); // ── Group E-b — U.S. government contracting readiness (opt-in; informational only) ── const _govPackOn = !!getBuildProfile().govContractingPack; addP('gov_naics', 'NAICS candidates (gov)', '', '⬡ Gov readiness'); addP('gov_readiness', 'Gov readiness checklist', '', '⬡ Gov readiness'); addP('gov_cap', 'Capabilities statement template', '', '⬡ Gov readiness'); addP('gov_rfp', 'RFP response outline', '', '⬡ Gov readiness'); if (!_govPackOn) { setP('gov_naics', 'sk', 'Skipped — enable “U.S. government contracting pack” under Build package'); setP('gov_readiness', 'sk', 'Skipped — enable “U.S. government contracting pack” under Build package'); setP('gov_cap', 'sk', 'Skipped — enable “U.S. government contracting pack” under Build package'); setP('gov_rfp', 'sk', 'Skipped — enable “U.S. government contracting pack” under Build package'); } else { setP('gov_naics', 'run'); setP('gov_readiness', 'run'); setP('gov_cap', 'run'); setP('gov_rfp', 'run'); const _govCtx = `Project: ${inf.name} Type: ${inf.type_label} Revenue model: ${inf.revenue_model} at ${inf.price} Build category: ${typeof V2_CATEGORY !== 'undefined' ? V2_CATEGORY : ''} Description (brief): ${(desc || '').slice(0, 1200)}`; await Promise.allSettled([ (async () => { try { const raw = await aiRaw(`${_govCtx} You are helping a founder prepare **informational** U.S. government-market materials. You must NOT invent real contract numbers, dollar amounts, incumbents, or specific past awards. If you give search examples, label them as **illustrative** only. Output a single markdown document for \`docs/NAICS-CANDIDATES.md\` with: ## Suggested NAICS codes (candidates only) - Table: Code | Title (short) | Why it might fit this business | Confidence (low/med) — **each row must say "confirm on Census NAICS"** - Propose **one primary** and **2–4 alternate** codes; if uncertain, say so. ## What NAICS is used for (brief) - SAM registration, reporting, statistics — not a "license to sell to government." ## Common mistakes - Picking a code because it "sounds impressive" vs. what you actually deliver. ## Next steps (checklist) - Verify codes on Census NAICS; align with accounting / insurance applications; update SAM when profile is created. Keep under ~700 words. No eligibility claims (no "you qualify as small business").`, 4000); if (raw?.length > 400) { files['docs/NAICS-CANDIDATES.md'] = `# NAICS code candidates — ${inf.name}\n\n${govContractingDisclaimerMd()}${raw}`; setP('gov_naics', 'ok', 'Done'); } else setP('gov_naics', 'sk', 'Skipped'); } catch (e) { setP('gov_naics', 'sk', e.message); } })(), (async () => { try { const raw = await aiRaw(`${_govCtx} Produce \`docs/GOV-READINESS.md\` — a **founder checklist** for exploring U.S. federal/state/local **contracting** (informational). Rules: - Link or name official systems only (SAM.gov, USASpending, FPDS as "where to look", state procurement portals conceptually). - Include sections: ## Is government a realistic channel for this business? (honest criteria), ## SAM.gov and unique entity ID (high level steps), ## Small business programs (8(a), WOSB, SDVOSB, HUBZone) — **describe what they are** and say eligibility requires **SBA / certifying partner** confirmation; never say the reader qualifies. - ## Certifications vs. registrations — plain language. - ## Researching opportunities without false precision — saved searches, NAICS/PSC-style filters as concepts; **do not** list fake solicitations. - ## Past performance and team credentials — what buyers look for; template ideas only. - ## Compliance awareness (not legal advice) — FAR/DFARS mentioned as "get counsel for regulated work"; CMMC mentioned only as "if you handle CUI, investigate." - ## Red flags — guaranteeing wins, paying for "set-aside guarantees," mixing personal and entity registrations. End with ## Official resources (bulleted links to sam.gov, usaspending.gov, sba.gov contracting pages, census NAICS). ~900 words max; dense bullets OK.`, 4500); if (raw?.length > 500) { files['docs/GOV-READINESS.md'] = `# U.S. government contracting readiness — ${inf.name}\n\n${govContractingDisclaimerMd()}${raw}`; setP('gov_readiness', 'ok', 'Done'); } else setP('gov_readiness', 'sk', 'Skipped'); } catch (e) { setP('gov_readiness', 'sk', e.message); } })(), (async () => { try { const raw = await aiRaw(`${_govCtx} Create \`docs/CAPABILITIES-STATEMENT.template.md\` — a **capabilities statement** shell used by many vendors. Use **double-curly placeholders** only, e.g. {{LEGAL_NAME}}, {{UEI}}, {{CAGE}}, {{PRIMARY_NAICS}}, {{WEBSITE}}, {{EMAIL}}, {{PHONE}}, {{CEO_NAME}}, {{PAST_PERF_SUMMARY}} — so owners can fill in after verifying data in SAM. Include: - Short instructions at top (1 paragraph). - Sections: Company overview, Core competencies (bullet list placeholders), Differentiators, **Certifications / registrations (placeholder — verify in SAM/SBA)**, Past performance (table template with {{PROJECT}}, {{AGENCY_TYPE}}, {{SCOPE}}, {{YEAR}} — **no fabricated projects** - One short **example** "competency bullet" row labeled **ILLUSTRATIVE — replace with your verified facts** No real DUNS/UEI numbers. ~600 words.`, 3500); if (raw?.length > 400) { files['docs/CAPABILITIES-STATEMENT.template.md'] = `# Capabilities statement — template — ${inf.name}\n\n${govContractingDisclaimerMd()}${raw}`; setP('gov_cap', 'ok', 'Done'); } else setP('gov_cap', 'sk', 'Skipped'); } catch (e) { setP('gov_cap', 'sk', e.message); } })(), (async () => { try { const raw = await aiRaw(`${_govCtx} Create \`docs/RFP-RESPONSE-OUTLINE.md\` — a **generic outline** for responding to government RFPs/RFQs (not a bid for a real solicitation). Rules: - No RFP number, no agency-specific false details, no invented deadlines. - Use ## sections: Understanding the requirement, Compliance matrix approach (shred RFP into requirements table), Technical approach (subheadings only), Management plan, Staffing and key personnel (resume placeholders), Past performance (how to cite **verified** projects), Pricing / basis of estimate (high level; "coordinate with finance and contracts counsel"), Quality assurance, Risk register, Subcontractors / teaming (if any), Required forms (acknowledge they vary by agency). - Short **"Before you submit"** checklist (legal review, pricing review, signatories). ~700 words.`, 4000); if (raw?.length > 400) { files['docs/RFP-RESPONSE-OUTLINE.md'] = `# RFP / RFQ response outline — ${inf.name}\n\n${govContractingDisclaimerMd()}${raw}`; setP('gov_rfp', 'ok', 'Done'); } else setP('gov_rfp', 'sk', 'Skipped'); } catch (e) { setP('gov_rfp', 'sk', e.message); } })() ]); } if (_govPackOn && files['MANUAL-ACTIONS.md'] != null && typeof files['MANUAL-ACTIONS.md'] === 'string' && files['docs/GOV-READINESS.md']) { files['MANUAL-ACTIONS.md'] += ` --- ## Optional: U.S. government contracting If you may sell to **federal, state, or local** agencies, use these **informational** repo files (verify everything on official sites — not from AI alone): 1. \`docs/GOV-READINESS.md\` — readiness checklist and official links 2. \`docs/NAICS-CANDIDATES.md\` — **candidate** NAICS codes to confirm on Census NAICS 3. \`docs/CAPABILITIES-STATEMENT.template.md\` — capabilities statement shell (fill with **verified** SAM data) 4. \`docs/RFP-RESPONSE-OUTLINE.md\` — generic response structure (no real solicitation implied) `; } // ── Group F — Go-to-Market (4 docs) ──────────── addP('channels', 'Channel Playbooks', '', '⬡ GTM Docs'); setP('channels', 'run'); addP('referral', 'Referral Program', '', '⬡ GTM Docs'); setP('referral', 'run'); addP('launch', 'Launch Checklist', '', '⬡ GTM Docs'); setP('launch', 'run'); addP('churn', 'Churn Reduction', '', '⬡ GTM Docs'); setP('churn', 'run'); const [channelsR, referralR, launchR, churnR] = await Promise.allSettled([ (async () => { try { const raw = await aiRaw(`Generate deep tactical channel playbooks for "${inf.name}" (${inf.type_label}). ${desc} Revenue model: ${inf.revenue_model} at ${inf.price}. Create detailed playbooks for the TOP 5 ACQUISITION CHANNELS: 1.
